title: Differentially Private Fine-tuning of Language Models

structured.summary: This paper proposes a meta-framework for differentially private fine-tuning of large pre-trained language models by updating only a small subset of parameters via parameter-efficient methods such as LoRA, adapters, and Compacter. The approach achieves state-of-the-art privacy-utility tradeoffs, reduces computational and memory overhead, and demonstrates that larger models better retain accuracy under differential privacy constraints, including the first private fine-tuning of GPT-2-XL.

strengths.point: Achieves strong privacy-utility tradeoffs on standard NLP benchmarks
strengths.evidence: DP LoRA on RoBERTa-Large achieves 90.3% average accuracy on GLUE at ε=6.7, close to non-private 93.4%, outperforming full fine-tuning and prior RGP methods.

strengths.point: Significant computational and memory efficiency gains
strengths.evidence: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning updates <1% of parameters, resulting in ~33% memory savings and ~8% speedup compared to RGP.

strengths.point: Demonstrates scalability to very large models under DP
strengths.evidence: First reported DP fine-tuning of GPT-2-XL (1.5B parameters) with competitive BLEU scores on generation tasks.

strengths.point: Provides a unified meta-framework encompassing multiple parameter-efficient methods
strengths.evidence: Framework abstracts LoRA, adapters, and Compacter as additive perturbations to frozen weights, enabling modular private fine-tuning.

weaknesses.point: Lacks comparison to other parameter-efficient DP methods like prefix-tuning or prompt-tuning
weaknesses.evidence: Paper compares LoRA, adapters, and Compacter but omits recent DP approaches such as prefix-tuning, limiting novelty claims.

weaknesses.point: Insufficient methodological details on privacy accounting and hyperparameters
weaknesses.evidence: No description of privacy accountant used or rationale for noise multiplier and clipping norm; missing DPSGD hyperparameters and module-specific settings.

weaknesses.point: Evaluation baselines not fully matched in model size or training setup
weaknesses.evidence: Comparison of DP LoRA on RoBERTa-Large to full fine-tuning and RGP on RoBERTa-Base may confound improvements due to model size.

weaknesses.point: Limited evaluation metrics and task diversity
weaknesses.evidence: Generation tasks evaluated only with BLEU; no human or alternative metrics; experiments restricted to English and standard NLP tasks.

weaknesses.point: No ablation or analysis on initialization sensitivity or combining parameter-efficient methods
weaknesses.evidence: Initialization of fine-tuning parameters not studied; no exploration of hybrid approaches combining LoRA, adapters, and Compacter.

weaknesses.point: Lack of code release and detailed reproducibility information
weaknesses.evidence: No mention of code or training scripts; missing critical hyperparameter details hinders reproducibility.

weaknesses.point: Ambiguity in meta-framework scope and insufficient explanation of Compacter
weaknesses.evidence: Unclear if framework supports methods beyond additive perturbations; minimal intuition provided for Compacter method.

weaknesses.point: No discussion of privacy-utility tradeoffs at very low ε or privacy risks from pre-trained models
weaknesses.evidence: Results at ε=0.1 not analyzed for practical utility; potential privacy leakage from pre-trained weights not addressed.

weaknesses.point: No runtime or memory cost breakdown by training component
weaknesses.evidence: Claims of efficiency gains lack detailed profiling to identify bottlenecks or optimization opportunities.

weaknesses.point: Limited generalizability beyond English and Transformer architectures
weaknesses.evidence: Experiments confined to English datasets and Transformer models; no evaluation on other languages, modalities, or architectures.

questions.question: How would the proposed meta-framework perform when incorporating other parameter-efficient DP methods such as prefix-tuning or prompt-tuning?
questions.motivation: Including these methods could strengthen novelty and provide a more comprehensive understanding of parameter-efficient DP fine-tuning.

questions.question: What is the sensitivity of the fine-tuning performance to the initialization of the trainable parameters θ, and how does this affect reproducibility?
questions.motivation: Understanding initialization effects is crucial for robustness and practical adoption of the method.

questions.question: Can the authors provide detailed privacy accounting methodology and hyperparameter settings to facilitate reproducibility?
questions.motivation: Transparency in privacy guarantees and training configurations is essential for verifying results and building upon this work.

questions.question: How does the method perform on more diverse tasks, languages, and model architectures beyond English Transformers?
questions.motivation: Evaluating generalizability is important to assess the broader applicability of the approach.

questions.question: What are the practical utility and limitations of the method at very strong privacy levels (e.g., ε=0.1), and how does accuracy degrade?
questions.motivation: Understanding performance in high-privacy regimes informs deployment decisions in sensitive applications.

critique_points.point_001: Lacks comparison to other parameter-efficient DP methods like prefix-tuning or prompt-tuning. Paper compares LoRA, adapters, and Compacter but omits recent DP approaches such as prefix-tuning, limiting novelty claims
critique_points.point_002: Insufficient methodological details on privacy accounting and hyperparameters. No description of privacy accountant used or rationale for noise multiplier and clipping norm; missing DPSGD hyperparameters and module-specific settings
critique_points.point_003: Evaluation baselines not fully matched in model size or training setup. Comparison of DP LoRA on RoBERTa-Large to full fine-tuning and RGP on RoBERTa-Base may confound improvements due to model size
critique_points.point_004: Limited evaluation metrics and task diversity. Generation tasks evaluated only with BLEU; no human or alternative metrics; experiments restricted to English and standard NLP tasks
critique_points.point_005: No ablation or analysis on initialization sensitivity or combining parameter-efficient methods. Initialization of fine-tuning parameters not studied; no exploration of hybrid approaches combining LoRA, adapters, and Compacter
critique_points.point_006: Lack of code release and detailed reproducibility information. No mention of code or training scripts; missing critical hyperparameter details hinders reproducibility
critique_points.point_007: Ambiguity in meta-framework scope and insufficient explanation of Compacter. Unclear if framework supports methods beyond additive perturbations; minimal intuition provided for Compacter method
critique_points.point_008: No discussion of privacy-utility tradeoffs at very low ε or privacy risks from pre-trained models. Results at ε=0.1 not analyzed for practical utility; potential privacy leakage from pre-trained weights not addressed
critique_points.point_009: No runtime or memory cost breakdown by training component. Claims of efficiency gains lack detailed profiling to identify bottlenecks or optimization opportunities
critique_points.point_010: Limited generalizability beyond English and Transformer architectures. Experiments confined to English datasets and Transformer models; no evaluation on other languages, modalities, or architectures
